elements.sent-2411-001: Time series classification is a relevant step supporting decision-making processes in various domains, and deep neural models have shown promising performance.
elements.sent-2411-002: Despite significant advancements in deep learning, the theoretical understanding of how and why complex architectures function remains limited, prompting the need for more interpretable models.
elements.sent-2411-003: Recently, the Kolmogorov-Arnold Networks (KANs) have been proposed as a more interpretable alternative.
elements.sent-2411-004: While KAN-related research is significantly rising, to date, the study of KAN architectures for time series classification has been limited.
elements.sent-2411-005: In this paper, we aim to conduct a comprehensive and robust exploration of the KAN architecture for time series classification on the UCR benchmark.
elements.sent-2411-006: More specifically, we look at a) how reference architectures for forecasting transfer to classification, at the b) hyperparameter and implementation influence on the classification performance in view of finding the one that performs best on the selected benchmark, the c) complexity trade-offs and d) interpretability advantages.
elements.sent-2411-007: Our results show that (1) Efficient KAN outperforms MLP in performance and computational efficiency, showcasing its suitability for tasks classification tasks.
elements.sent-2411-008: Time series classification is a relevant step supporting decision-making processes in various domains.
elements.sent-2411-009: In healthcare for example, time series classification enables the diagnosis and monitoring of conditions by analyzing patterns in physiological signals such as electrocardiograms or brain activity \cite{wang2022systematic .}
elements.sent-2411-010: In finance, it enhances risk management and fraud detection by classifying trading behaviors and transaction patterns \cite{devaki2014credit .}
elements.sent-2411-011: By classifying activities based on sensor data in human activity recognition, it supports personalized recommendations and safety monitoring \cite{yang2015deep .}
elements.sent-2411-012: In remote sensing, it contributes to environmental monitoring and land use classification by categorizing satellite or aerial imagery data over time \cite{gomez2016optical .}
elements.sent-2411-013: In these fields, accurate time series classification plays a central role in advancing predictive and analytic capabilities that drive critical outcomes.
elements.sent-2411-014: Unlike time series forecasting, which aims to predict future data points, time series classification aims to provide accurate interpretation and categorization of temporal data sequences by assigning a categorical label, class label, to each sequence \cite{bagnall2024hands .}
elements.sent-2411-015: Following a well-accepted survey \cite{ismail2019deep that revealed the lack of investigation of deep learning techniques for time series classification, significant progress has been achieved in related research with hundreds of new works, some of them surpassing the non-deep state of the art \cite{middlehurst2024bake .}}
elements.sent-2411-016: According to the most recent and extensive benchmarking work on 100+ datasets from the UCR archive, the most extensive benchmark for time series classification, the state of the art F1 score of 0.886 is achieved by Hive-Cote2 \cite{bagnall2015time \cite{middlehurst2021hive , a large ensemble with considerable training time.}}
elements.sent-2411-017: The non-deep and non-hybrid classification methods that are more explainable at both feature engineering and decision making steps achieve an F1 of up to 0.869 while the deep architectures reach around 0.88 F1 score.
elements.sent-2411-018: Foundation models for time series, based on transformers, are yet to be studied on cross-domain benchmarks such as UCR~\cite{liang2024foundation .}
elements.sent-2411-019: Despite the disruptive advancements introduced by breakthroughs in machine vision and natural language processing over the last decades, the theoretical understanding of why and how complex deep architectures function has lagged behind~\cite{bachmann2024scaling .}
elements.sent-2411-020: This has prompted researchers to step back and reconsider fundamental and mathematically simpler architectures, such as MLPs~\cite{teney2024neural .}
elements.sent-2411-021: Furthermore, the influence of the academic scientific community in this new era of AI is decreasing~\cite{Ahmed2023 , while non-AI scientific communities are concerned with the interpretability of deep models~\cite{bachmann2024scaling .}}
elements.sent-2411-022: Recently, the Kolmogorov-Arnold Networks (KANs)~\cite{liu2024kan have been proposed as addressing the limitations of traditional neural networks.}
elements.sent-2411-023: KANs have demonstrated to be interpretable, even enabling symbolic regression, and have comparable performance with MLPs on small scale and science tasks with shallower architectures.
elements.sent-2411-024: Additional advantages have been explored for data fitting and solving partial differential equations \cite{liu2024kan2 .}
elements.sent-2411-025: The main critique of the original KAN work is concerned with the fairness of the comparison, triggering substantial research into better understanding their overall performance beyond the initial small scale scientific tasks.
elements.sent-2411-026: Subsequently it has been shown that their performance advantage in non-scientific ML tasks such as vision, natural language and audio processing does not hold \cite{yu2024kan .}
elements.sent-2411-027: Additional work reveals their reduced effectiveness on functions with noise \cite{shen2024reduced while other works investigate the suitability of replacing the spline functions with wavelets \cite{bozorgasl2024wav .}}
elements.sent-2411-028: The performance of KANs vs MLPs on graph learning tasks is compared in \cite{bresson2024kagnns .}
elements.sent-2411-029: Their preliminary results reveal that while KANs are on-par with MLPs in classification tasks, they seem to have a clear advantage in the graph regression tasks.
elements.sent-2411-030: While KAN-related is significantly rising, new scientific works published almost weekly, to date, the study of KAN architectures for time series has been limited.
elements.sent-2411-031: Very early forecasting studies encompass Temporal KANs (TKANs)~\cite{genet2024tkan and Temporal Kolmogorov Arnold Transformer \cite{genet2024temporal for notional trades, mixture of experts \cite{han2024kan4tsf for various domains including traffic and weather, satellite traffic \cite{vaca2024kolmogorov and integrating convolutional layers with KANs to improve time-series forecasting~\cite{livieris2024c .}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-032: Furthermore, with respect to time series classification, studies on their robustness are emerging \cite{dong2024kolmogorov .}
elements.sent-2411-033: In this paper we aim to conduct a comprehensive and robust exploration of the KAN architecture for time series classification on the UCR benchmark.
elements.sent-2411-034: More specifically, we look at a) how the existing architectures for forecasting \cite{vaca2024kolmogorov transfer to classification, at the b) hyperparameter and implementation influence on the classification performance in view of finding the one that performs best on the selected benchmark, the c) complexity trade-ffs and d) interpretability advantages.}
elements.sent-2411-035: The contributions of this paper are as follows.
elements.sent-2411-036: A study on the suitability of existing KAN architectures, previously proposed for timeseries forecasting, for classification tasks on the UCR dataset.
elements.sent-2411-037: A hyperparameter impact analysis of two KAN implementations, analyzing how variations in grid size, network depth, and node configurations impact classification performance.
elements.sent-2411-038: The analysis leads to finding the best performing KAN configuration for the considered problem.
elements.sent-2411-039: The performance and computational complexity comparison of the original KAN, the Efficient KAN implementation, and Multi-Layer Perceptrons (MLPs) on time series classification tasks reveals EfficientKAN's superior stability across grid sizes depths and layer configurations.
elements.sent-2411-040: We confirm KAN’s interpretability by diving deeper into the learnt feature importance and activations functions vs SHAP importance.
elements.sent-2411-041: The paper is structured as follows.
elements.sent-2411-042: We discuss related work in Section \ref{sec:related .}
elements.sent-2411-043: Section \ref{sec:statement outlines the problem statement, followed by Section \ref{sec:methodology detailing methodological aspects.}}
elements.sent-2411-044: A comprehensive analysis of the results is presented in Section \ref{sec:results .}
elements.sent-2411-045: Lastly, the paper concludes with Section \ref{sec:conclusion .}
elements.sent-2411-046: Kolmogorov-Arnold Networks (KANs) emerged as an innovative alternative to traditional Multi-Layer Perceptrons (MLPs), inspired by the Kolmogorov-Arnold representation theorem.
elements.sent-2411-047: KANs were shown to outperform MLPs in both accuracy and interpretability on small-scale scientific tasks.
elements.sent-2411-048: Their design, which models complex functions and patterns with fewer parameters, demonstrated potential to aid in mathematical and physical discoveries \cite{liu2024kan .}
elements.sent-2411-049: Building on these findings, subsequent research \cite{liu2024kan2 further developed KANs to bridge the gap between artificial intelligence and scientific research.}
elements.sent-2411-050: By allowing KANs to identify relevant features, reveal modular structures, and discover symbolic formulas, KAN 2.0 \cite{liu2024kan2 introduced a bidirectional approach that not only incorporates scientific knowledge into KANs but also enables KANs to extract interpretable scientific insights from data.}
elements.sent-2411-051: However, KANs have been shown to be highly sensitive to noise, prompting the introduction of oversampling and denoising techniques, such as kernel filtering with diffusion maps, to mitigate noise effects\cite{shen2024reduced .}
elements.sent-2411-052: To improve the handling of noisy data, architectural extensions have additionally explored the replacement of splines with wavelets \cite{bozorgasl2024wav , enhancing KAN’s robustness and adaptability in broader applications.}
elements.sent-2411-053: KANs and MLPs were also investigated beyond scientific datasets in various domains in a controlled study with consistent parameters and FLOPs \cite{yu2024kan .}
elements.sent-2411-054: While MLPs outperformed KANs in most areas, KANs retained a distinct advantage in symbolic formula representation due to B-spline activation functions.
elements.sent-2411-055: Replacing MLPs' activations with B-splines improved their performance, suggesting KAN-inspired enhancements for MLPs.
elements.sent-2411-056: However, KANs faced memory stability issues in continual learning, requiring specialized tuning or hybrid models.
elements.sent-2411-057: In graph learning tasks \cite{bresson2024kagnns , experiments on node classification, graph classification, and graph regression datasets indicated that KANs are on par with MLPs in classification but exhibit an advantage in graph regression tasks.}
elements.sent-2411-058: In a real-world satellite traffic forecasting task, KANs achieved comparable or superior accuracy to MLPs while using fewer parameters, showcasing their potential in predictive analytics \cite{vaca2024kolmogorov .}
elements.sent-2411-059: This application framed forecasting as a supervised learning problem with specific input-output mappings across time steps, using a GEO satellite traffic dataset.
elements.sent-2411-060: The success of KANs in this setting prompted specialized variants, such as Temporal KAN (T-KAN) and Multivariate Temporal KAN (MT-KAN) \cite{xu2024kolmogorov .}
elements.sent-2411-061: T-KAN targets univariate time series, capturing nonlinear relationships with symbolic regression, while MT-KAN model dependencies between multiple variables for improved accuracy in multivariate settings.
elements.sent-2411-062: Further advancements, like Temporal Kolmogorov-Arnold Networks (TKANs), incorporated LSTM-inspired memory layers, excelling in tasks such as cryptocurrency trading volume forecasting.
elements.sent-2411-063: Temporal Kolmogorov-Arnold Transformer (TKAT) added self-attention mechanisms, outperforming conventional transformers in interpretability and precision in multivariate time series forcasting \cite{genet2024tkan, genet2024temporal .}
elements.sent-2411-064: Signature-Weighted KANs (SigKAN) extended these innovations by integrating path signatures, making them robust for market volume prediction \cite{inzirillo2024sigkan .}
elements.sent-2411-065: The Reversible Mixture of KAN Experts (RMoK) model, another KAN-based approach, introduced a mixture-of-experts structure to assign variables to KAN experts, achieving strong performance in time series forecasting tasks by leveraging temporal feature weights to explain data periodicity \cite{han2024kan4tsf .}
elements.sent-2411-066: To enhance time series classification robustness, hybrid models that combine KAN and MLP architectures were further explored \cite{dong2024kolmogorov .}
elements.sent-2411-067: The study employed Efficient KAN as the primary implementation of KAN rather than the original implementation.
elements.sent-2411-068: Two hybrid configurations, KAN MLP (KAN with an MLP as the final layer) and MLP KAN (MLP with a KAN as the final layer), were tested across the UCR datasets, and both hybrids achieved performance comparable to traditional KAN and MLP models.
elements.sent-2411-069: Notably, MLP KAN demonstrated increased resilience against adversarial attacks, with this robustness attributed to the lower Lipschitz constant of KAN layers, suggesting that combining KAN with MLP structures can strengthen adversarial resistance in neural networks, thus opening possibilities for more secure and reliable models.
elements.sent-2411-070: Rather than focusing on robustness and hybrid architecture, this study aims at understanding in depth the performance and interpretability of KANs for time series classification.
elements.sent-2411-071: In this paper we analyze the potential of KAN for solving univariate time-series classification problems.
elements.sent-2411-072: A univariate time series \( X \) consists of a sequence of observations $[x_{1 , x_{2 , \ldots, x_{T ]$, where each $x_t, t \in \{1, 2, ..., T\}$ represents a value observed at time \( t \) representing an ordered set of real values \cite{ismail2019deep .}}}}
elements.sent-2411-073: A class label \( Y \) is a categorical variable associated with a time series \( X \), indicating the class that the time series belongs to.
elements.sent-2411-074: The number of distinct classes is denoted by the cardinality $C$ of \( Y \), meaning \( Y \) can take a value from the set $\{y_1, y_2, \ldots, y_C\}$.
elements.sent-2411-075: When $C=2$, this corresponds to a binary classification problem, while $C>2$ represents a multiclass classification problem.
elements.sent-2411-076: As shown in Fig. \ref{fig:ts_classes , where \( C = 3 \), each plot corresponds to a different class, illustrating the distinct patterns for \( y_1 \), \( y_2 \), and \( y_3 \).}
elements.sent-2411-077: A dataset $D = \{(X_1, Y_1), (X_2, Y_2), \ldots, (X_N, Y_N)\}$ consists of pairs $(X_i, Y_i) $, where $X_i, i \in \{1, 2, 3..., N\}$ represents a univariate time series, and $Y_i$ is the corresponding class label.
elements.sent-2411-078: The task of time series classification involves training a classifier on a dataset \(D\) to map each input \(X_i\) to a probability distribution across the possible class labels \cite{ismail2019deep . \begin{equation}f(D) \approx Y\end{equation} Specifically, instead of assigning a single class label to each time series, the classifier outputs a probability distribution across all possible classes.}
elements.sent-2411-079: That is, for each time series \( X_i \), the classifier predicts a vector of probabilities \( P(Y_i = y_c \mid X_i) \) for each class \( c \in \{1, 2, \dots, C\} \).
elements.sent-2411-080: The classification function $f$ can be realized through a multitude of exiting approaches \cite{middlehurst2024bake , however we investigate the recently proposed Kolomogrow Arnold Network (KAN) \cite{liu2024kan promising increased interpretability and potential computational benefits compared to standard Multilayer Perceptron (MLP).}}
elements.figure-2411-001: \includegraphics[width=\linewidth]{ts_classes.pdf} \caption{Example of time series data plotted by class, with time steps on the x-axis and observed values $x_t$ on the y-axis. Figure depicts SmoothSubspace dataset from the UCR repository.} \label{fig:ts_classes}
elements.sent-2411-081: The architecture of a KAN is depicted in Fig. \ref{fig:kan_arch .}
elements.sent-2411-082: It can be seen from the figure that, for an input instance \( X_i \), the network outputs \( Y_i \) label assignment probability vector.
elements.sent-2411-083: The structure of a KAN can be represented as $[n_1,…,n_{L+1 ]$, where $L$ signifies the total number of layers in the KAN.}
elements.sent-2411-084: A deeper KAN can be thus formulated through the composition of $L$ layers as: \begin{equation}Y = \text{KAN}(X) = (\Phi_L \circ \Phi_{L-1} \circ \dots \circ \Phi_1)X \label{eq:eq_phi_composition}\end{equation} Each layer of a KAN is represented by a matrix where each entry is an activation function.
elements.sent-2411-085: If there is a layer with $d_{in}$ nodes and its neighboring layer with $d_{out}$ nodes, the layer can be represented as a $d_{in \times d_{out}$ matrix of activation functions (Eq. \ref{activation_func ). \begin{equation}\Phi = \{\phi_{q,p}\}, \; p = 1, 2, \dots, d_{in}, \; q = 1, 2, \dots, d_{out}\end{equation} Unlike traditional MLPs, where activation functions are applied at the nodes themselves, KAN places them at the edges between the nodes.}}
elements.sent-2411-086: KAN employs the SiLU activation function in combination with B-splines to enhance its expressiveness.
elements.sent-2411-087: This setup allows the edges to control the transformations between layers, while the nodes perform simple summation operations. \begin{equation}\label{activation_func} \phi(x) = w_b silu(x) + w_s \, \text{spline}(x)\end{equation} where SiLU is defined as $\text{silu (x) = x/(1+e^{-x )$.}}
elements.sent-2411-088: This activation function allows for smooth, non-linear transformations, which helps the network capture complex patterns in time series data more effectively.
elements.sent-2411-089: B-splines are smooth polynomial functions of degree (order) $k$, which approximate data using control points.
elements.sent-2411-090: The order $k$ controls the smoothness of the spline.
elements.sent-2411-091: Commonly, $k=3$ is used for cubic splines.
elements.sent-2411-092: Each spline operates over a defined grid $G$, which divides the input space into smaller intervals.
elements.sent-2411-093: The grid is determined by a set of grid points that define the segments of the spline.
elements.sent-2411-094: Increasing the number of grid points, increases spline resolution, enabling more fine grained approximation of the underlying univariate function.
elements.sent-2411-095: A B-spline of order $k$ requires $G+k$ basis functions to represent the spline over the grid.
elements.sent-2411-096: For each input (node in a layer), the evaluation of a B-spline of order $k$ thus involves computing $G+k-1$ basis functions and performing a weighted sum with the corresponding control points. \begin{equation}\text{spline}(x) = \sum_{i=0}^{G+k-1} c_i B_i(x)\end{equation} To better understand the performance of the models evaluated, it is crucial to explain their decision-making processes.
elements.sent-2411-097: Although black-box models achieve high accuracy, they often lack interpretability.
elements.sent-2411-098: KANs offer a shift in the interpretability paradigm by allowing visualization of the model structure along with its learned B-splines as shown in Fig.~\ref{fig:kan_arch .}
elements.sent-2411-099: Interpretability is further enhanced by model pruning that simplifies visual representation by removing less important connections within the model, which were determined through their significance scores.
elements.sent-2411-100: These scores identify critical connections, which highlight the most important features for the decision of the model.
elements.sent-2411-101: This approach provides insights into how KANs process data and make predictions by focusing on the most influential connections within the structure of the model.
elements.sent-2411-102: The number of learnable parameters in a KAN layer is determined by its architecture, which includes contributions from the B-spline control points, shortcut path weights, B-spline weights, and bias terms.
elements.sent-2411-103: As discussed in \cite{yu2024kan , the number of learnable parameters for each KAN layer is: \begin{equation}\text{Parameters} = (d_{in} \cdot d_{out}) \cdot (G + k + 3) + d_{out}\end{equation} Where \(d_{in}\) and \(d_{out}\) represent the input and output dimensions of the layer.}
elements.sent-2411-104: This is consistent with the findings in the \cite{liu2024kan , which also emphasize the role of learnable control points and weights associated with the spline functions.}
elements.sent-2411-105: The number of FLOPs in a Kolmogorov–Arnold Network (KAN) is highly dependent on the specific implementation and the way the network is compiled on various processor architectures.
elements.sent-2411-106: Different hardware platforms optimize these computations differently, leading to variations in practical FLOPs performance.
elements.sent-2411-107: However, we adopt the theoretical FLOPs computation model as outlined in \cite{yu2024kan for consistency and comparison purposes.}
elements.sent-2411-108: The total FLOPs for a KAN layer come from three parts: the B-spline transformation, the shortcut path, and the merging of the two branches.
elements.sent-2411-109: Using the De Boor-Cox iterative formulation for B-splines, the FLOPs for one KAN layer in the official KAN implementation is given by \cite{yu2024kan : \begin{equation}\begin{split} \text{FLOPs} = \ \text{FLOPs of non-linear function} \cdot d_{\text{in}} + (d_{\text{in}} \cdot d_{\text{out}}) \cdot \left[ 9 \cdot k \cdot (G + 1.5 \cdot k) \right. \left. + 2 \cdot G - 2.5 \cdot k + 3 \right] \end{split} \label{eq:kan_flops}\end{equation} FLOPs of one forward pass $FP$ through a network with uniform hidden layers of size $d_{in \cdot d_{out = M \cdot M$ are calculated as in Eq. \ref{eq:kan_fp_flops : \begin{equation}\begin{split} N_{FP}= \ \text{FLOPs of non-linear function} \cdot T + T \cdot M \cdot \big[ 9 \cdot k \cdot (G + 1.5 \cdot k) \quad\ + 2 \cdot G - 2.5 \cdot k + 3 \big] + (L - 2) \cdot \big( \text{FLOPs of non-linear function} \cdot M \quad\ + M^2 \cdot \big[ 9 \cdot k \cdot (G + 1.5 \cdot k) \quad\quad\ + 2 \cdot G - 2.5 \cdot k + 3 \big] \big) + \text{FLOPs of non-linear function} \cdot M + M \cdot C \cdot \big[ 9 \cdot k \cdot (G + 1.5 \cdot k) \quad\ + 2 \cdot G - 2.5 \cdot k + 3 \big] \end{split} \label{eq:kan_fp_flops}\end{equation}.}}}}
elements.formula-2411-007: \begin{equation} FLOPs = \ FLOPs of non-linear function \cdot d_{in} + (d_{in} \cdot d_{out}) \cdot \left[ 9 \cdot k \cdot (G + 1.5 \cdot k) \right. \left. + 2 \cdot G - 2.5 \cdot k + 3 \right]  \label{eq:kan_flops}\end{equation}
elements.formula-2411-008: \begin{equation} N_{FP}= \ FLOPs of non-linear function \cdot T + T \cdot M \cdot \big[ 9 \cdot k \cdot (G + 1.5 \cdot k) \quad\ + 2 \cdot G - 2.5 \cdot k + 3 \big] + (L - 2) \cdot \big( FLOPs of non-linear function \cdot M \quad\ + M^2 \cdot \big[ 9 \cdot k \cdot (G + 1.5 \cdot k) \quad\quad\ + 2 \cdot G - 2.5 \cdot k + 3 \big] \big) + FLOPs of non-linear function \cdot M + M \cdot C \cdot \big[ 9 \cdot k \cdot (G + 1.5 \cdot k) \quad\ + 2 \cdot G - 2.5 \cdot k + 3 \big]  \label{eq:kan_fp_flops}\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-110: A Multilayer Perceptron (MLP) is a type of feed-forward artificial neural network, used in various machine learning tasks, including time series classification.
elements.sent-2411-111: MLPs are inspired by the universal approximation theorem, which asserts that such networks can approximate any continuous function given sufficient depth (number of layers) and width (number of neurons per layer).
elements.sent-2411-112: The architecture of a MLP is depicted in Fig. \ref{fig:mlp_arch .}
elements.sent-2411-113: The MLP consists of multiple layers of nodes (neurons): input layer with $T$ neurons, $K$ hidden layers (layers which are not directly connected to the environment are called hidden \cite{popescu2009multilayer ) and output layer, consisting of $C$ output neurons.}
elements.sent-2411-114: Each neuron in a layer processes the input it receives from the previous layer by calculating a weighted sum of the inputs, adding a bias term, and then passing the result through an activation function.
elements.sent-2411-115: For a neuron with $n$ inputs, its output is given by the Eq.: \begin{equation}neuron = a\left( \sum_{i=1}^{n} weight_i \cdot input_i + bias \right)\end{equation} where $a$ represents the activation function.
elements.sent-2411-116: A commonly used activation function in MLPs is the Rectified Linear Unit (ReLU), defined as $\text{ReLU (z) = \max(0, z)$.}
elements.sent-2411-117: This structure allows MLPs to model complex, non-linear relationships between inputs and outputs.
elements.sent-2411-118: In time series classification, the input to the MLP $X$, is a fixed-length vector representing the time series data.
elements.sent-2411-119: The network learns to map this input vector to a probability distribution across the possible class labels, where each neuron $p_c, c \in \{1, 2, \dots, C\}$ in the final output layer represents the probability of the input belonging to a specific class $y_c$.
elements.sent-2411-120: The number of learnable parameters in an MLP is determined by the connections between neurons across layers.
elements.sent-2411-121: For a fully connected layer with $d_{in}$ input neurons and $d_{out}$ output neurons, the number of learnable parameters \cite{yu2024kan is: \begin{equation}Parameters = (d_{in} × d_{out})+ d_{out}\end{equation}.}
elements.sent-2411-122: In a fully connected MLP, each connection between two neurons $wx+b$ performs a weighted sum $w \cdot x$ and adds bias $b$, resulting in 1 multiplication and 1 addition.
elements.sent-2411-124: For a neuron with $d_{\text{in }$ inputs, calculating the output requires $2 \times d_{\text{in }$ FLOPs (for weights) and 1 FLOP for the bias, resulting in a total of $2 \times d_{\text{in + 1$ FLOPs.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-125: If there are $d_{\text{out }$ output neurons, the total FLOPs for the layer is $d_{\text{out \times (2 \times d_{\text{in + 1)$.}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-126: We consider fully connected MLP which has input layer with $T$ neurons, $K$ hidden layers, with $M$ neurons in each, and $C$ neurons in output layer.
elements.sent-2411-127: FLOPs for forward propagation are therefore calculated as \begin{equation}N_{FP} = (M + 2 M T) + (K-1)(M + 2 M^2) + (M + 2 M C) \label{eq:mlp_flops}\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-128: In this section we elaborate on the methodology adopted for this study ($https://github.com/irina-b1/KAN_TS$).
elements.sent-2411-129: First we provide considerations on data and preprocessing, followed by reference model configurations, training process, hyperparamater, complexity and interpretability analysis.
elements.sent-2411-130: In this study, the UCR (University of California, Riverside)\cite{dau2019ucr dataset archive time series classification benchmark is utilized.}
elements.sent-2411-131: The datasets were selected based on their completeness, since some datasets include time series instances containing missing values.
elements.sent-2411-132: To ensure that an adequate amount of data remained for both the training and testing phases, these datasets were excluded, resulting in total of 117 datasets used.
elements.sent-2411-133: In terms of size, the datasets vary significantly, with some containing 16 time series instances, while others encompassed up to 8,926 instances.
elements.sent-2411-134: The input lengths of these time series also showed considerable diversity, ranging from 15 to 2,844 time steps per instance in different datasets.
elements.sent-2411-135: Furthermore, the datasets included varying numbers of classes for classification tasks, with scenarios ranging from binary classifications with 2 classes to multiclass problems with up to 60 distinct classes.
elements.sent-2411-136: The first step in data preprocessing was normalization, using `StandardScaler` from the scikit-learn library.
elements.sent-2411-137: Each time series \( X \) in the dataset \( D \) was standardized by applying the transformation \( x_{t,\text{scaled = \frac{x_t - \mu_t {\sigma_t \), where \( \mu_t \) and \( \sigma_t \) are the mean and standard deviation for the value at time \( t \) across all time series in \( D \).}}}}
elements.sent-2411-138: This ensures that each time point \( t \) has a mean of 0 and a standard deviation of 1, allowing for consistent scaling across the dataset.
elements.sent-2411-139: This normalization is crucial for ensuring that the input features are on a comparable scale, which helps in efficient training.
elements.sent-2411-140: For training, each dataset was partitioned into into a training dataset and a validation dataset in an 80:20 ratio.
elements.sent-2411-141: The test datasets are provided separately by default.
elements.sent-2411-142: The training process across all models used the Adam optimizer with a learning rate of $0.001$.
elements.sent-2411-143: However, instead of the mean absolute error (MAE) loss function used for regression tasks in the original work, we employed the cross-entropy loss function to address the classification nature of our problem.
elements.sent-2411-144: Additionally, we introduced $L1$ regularization with a weight factor of $0.1$.
elements.sent-2411-145: Similarly, in the MLP models, we used the same Adam optimizer, learning rate ($0.001$), and cross-entropy loss function as in the KAN models, for the same reasons.
elements.sent-2411-146: However, we applied L2 regularization with a weight factor of 1, selected through empirical tuning, instead of L1 regularization.
elements.sent-2411-147: To ensure robustness and mitigate the effects of random initialization, each model was trained on 5 different random seeds.
elements.sent-2411-148: The results presented are averages of these 5 runs.
elements.sent-2411-149: The models were trained for 500 epochs using batch size 16.
elements.sent-2411-150: The original KAN library \cite{liu2024kan was modified to enable GPU support, allowing the experiments to leverage the computational power of GPUs rather than relying solely on CPU execution.}
elements.sent-2411-151: Thus, all evaluations involving KAN models were conducted on an NVIDIA A100 80GB GPU.
elements.sent-2411-152: For performance evaluation, we use precision, recall and F1 score.
elements.sent-2411-153: The precision measures how many instances predicted as a certain class actually belong to that class, expressed as: $\text{Precision = \frac{\text{TP {\text{TP + \text{FP }$, where TP (true positives) represents the number of correctly identified positive instances, and FP (false positives) refers to the number of instances incorrectly identified as positive.}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-154: Recall measures how many instances of some class were correctly detected, given by: $\text{Recall = \frac{\text{TP {\text{TP + \text{FN }$, where FN (false negatives) represents the number of actual positive instances that the model failed to identify as positive.}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-155: The F1 score is expressed as: $F1 = 2 \times \frac{\text{Precision \times \text{Recall {\text{Precision + \text{Recall }$, where higher values indicate better performance in balancing precision and recall (better classification).}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-156: The goal is to gain insights into the generalization capabilities and transfer potential of these architectures from regression to classification problems.

elements.sent-2411-159: It replaces input-based L1 regularization with weight-based L1, improving efficiency.

elements.sent-2411-181: Furthermore, to gain further insight into the decision making of KAN, global SHapley Additive exPlanations (SHAP) values~\cite{lundberg2017unified were used.}
elements.sent-2411-182: SHAP offers a model-agnostic framework for global interpretability by quantifying the average contribution of each feature to the model's predictions across the entire dataset.
elements.sent-2411-183: The contribution of a feature $x_i$ is represented by its Shapley value, $\phi_i$, which is computed based on cooperative game theory.
elements.sent-2411-184: For a model the prediction for an instance $X$ is expressed as: \begin{equation}SHAP = \phi_0 + \sum_{i=1}^M \phi_i\end{equation} where $\phi_0$ is the model’s baseline expected value across the dataset, and $\phi_i$ is the Shapley value of feature $x_i$, and M represents the total number of features.
elements.sent-2411-185: By aggregating these values SHAP enables insights into the global decisions of the model.
elements.sent-2411-186: In addition SHAP is also utilized to compare the feature importance of KANs to the MLP architecture.

elements.sent-2411-305: To achieve that, we first investigated the transferability of reference Kolmogorov-Arnold Networks (KANs) from regression to classification tasks followed by key hyperparameter search on two KAN implementations in view of finding the best architecture for the task at hand and concluding with an interpretability analysis.

elements.sent-2411-308: Efficient KAN proved more stable than KAN across grid sizes, depths, and layer configurations, particularly with lower learning rates.
elements.sent-2411-309: The interpretability of KANs was confirmed through SHAP analysis and B-spline visualization, highlighting their potential for transparent decision-making.

elements.sent-2411-318: Time series classification is a relevant step supporting decision-making processes in various domains, and deep neural models have shown promising performance in this respect.
elements.sent-2411-319: Recently, the Kolmogorov-Arnold Networks (KANs) have been proposed as a more interpretable alternative to deep learning.
elements.sent-2411-320: In this paper, we aim to conduct a comprehensive and robust exploration of the KAN architecture for time series classification utilising 117 datasets from UCR benchmark archive, from multiple different domains.
elements.sent-2411-321: More specifically, we investigate a) the transferability of reference architectures designed for regression to classification tasks, b) identifying the hyperparameter and implementation configurations for an architecture that best generalizes across 117 datasets, c) the associated complexity trade-offs and d) evaluate KANs interpretability.
elements.sent-2411-322: Our results demonstrate that (1) the Efficient KAN outperforms MLPs in both performance and training times, showcasing its suitability for classification tasks.
elements.sent-2411-323: For instance, in healthcare time series classification enables the diagnosis and monitoring of conditions by analyzing patterns in physiological signals such as electrocardiograms or brain activity \cite{wang2022systematic .}
elements.sent-2411-324: In these fields, accurate time series classification plays a central role in advancing predictive and analytic capabilities that drive critical decisions.
elements.sent-2411-325: Unlike time series forecasting, which aims to predict future data points, time series classification aims to provide accurate interpretation and categorization of temporal data sequences by assigning a categorical label or a class label to each sequence \cite{bagnall2024hands .}
elements.sent-2411-326: According to the most recent and extensive benchmarking work on more than 100 datasets from the UCR archive, the most extensive benchmark for time series classification, the state of the art F1 score of 0.886 is achieved by Hive-Cote2 \cite{bagnall2015time \cite{middlehurst2021hive , a large ensemble with considerable training time.}}
elements.sent-2411-327: The non-deep and non-hybrid classification methods that are more explainable at both feature engineering and decision making steps achieve F1 of up to 0.869 while the deep architectures reach around 0.88 F1 score.
elements.sent-2411-328: The main critique of the original KAN work is concerned with the fairness of the comparison, triggering substantial research into better understanding of their overall performance beyond the initial small scale scientific tasks.
elements.sent-2411-329: While KAN-related research is significantly rising, with new scientific works published almost weekly, the study of KAN architectures for time series has been limited to date.
elements.sent-2411-330: Very early forecasting studies encompass Temporal KANs (TKANs)~\cite{genet2024tkan , Temporal Kolmogorov Arnold Transformer \cite{genet2024temporal and mixture-of-experts \cite{han2024kan4tsf for various domains from traffic and weather to satellite traffic \cite{vaca2024kolmogorov and integrating convolutional layers with KANs to improve time-series forecasting~\cite{livieris2024c .}}}}}

elements.sent-2411-344: Each layer of a KAN is represented by a matrix where each entry is an activation function.
elements.sent-2411-345: If there is a layer with $d_{in}$ nodes and its neighboring layer with $d_{out}$ nodes, the layer can be represented as a $d_{in \times d_{out}$ matrix of activation functions: The structure of a KAN can be represented as $[n_1,…,n_{L+1 ]$, where $L$ signifies the total number of layers in the KAN.}}
elements.sent-2411-346: A deeper KAN can be thus formulated through the composition of $L$ layers as: \begin{equation}Y = \text{KAN}(X) = (\Phi_L \circ \Phi_{L-1} \circ \dots \circ \Phi_1)X. \label{eq:eq_phi_composition1}\end{equation} \begin{equation}\Phi = \{\phi_{q,p}\}, \; p = 1, 2, \dots, d_{in}, \; q = 1, 2, \dots, d_{out}. \label{eq:eq_phi_composition2}\end{equation} Unlike traditional MLPs, where activation functions are applied at the nodes themselves, KAN places them at the edges between the nodes.
elements.sent-2411-347: KAN employs the SiLU activation function in combination with B-splines to enhance its expressiveness, as per Eq.~\ref{activation_func .}
elements.sent-2411-348: This setup allows the edges to control the transformations between layers, while the nodes perform simple summation operations. \begin{equation}\phi(x) = w_b silu(x) + w_s \, \text{spline}(x). \label{activation_func}\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-350: Increasing the number of grid points increases spline resolution, enabling more fine grained approximation of the underlying univariate function.
elements.sent-2411-351: For each input (node in a layer), the evaluation of a B-spline of order $k$ thus involves computing $G+k-1$ basis functions and performing a weighted sum with the corresponding control points. \begin{equation}\text{spline}(x) = \sum_{i=0}^{G+k-1} c_i B_i(x)\end{equation} To better understand the performance of the evaluated models, it is crucial to explain their decision-making processes.
elements.sent-2411-352: As discussed in \cite{yu2024kan , the number of learnable parameters for each KAN layer is: \begin{equation}\text{Parameters} = (d_{in} \cdot d_{out}) \cdot (G + k + 3) + d_{out} ,\end{equation} where \(d_{in}\) and \(d_{out}\) represent the input and output dimensions of the layer.}
elements.sent-2411-353: This is consistent with findings in \cite{liu2024kan , which also emphasize the role of learnable control points and weights associated with the spline functions.}

elements.subsection-2411-008: Comparison of KAN to MLP for time series classification
elements.sent-2411-355: A Multilayer Perceptron (MLP) is a very well known type of feed-forward neural network widely used for time series classification.
elements.sent-2411-356: KANs and MLPs share several architectural principles but differ significantly in how they implement non-linearity and function approximation.
elements.sent-2411-357: Both are fully connected neural networks in which each layer's nodes are densely connected to the next.
elements.sent-2411-358: The input layer in each architecture corresponds to the length of the input time series, and the output layer produces a probability distribution over class labels, allowing for multiclass classification.
elements.sent-2411-359: However, their treatment of activation differs notably.
elements.sent-2411-360: In MLPs, activation functions are applied at the neurons (i.e. nodes).
elements.sent-2411-361: In KANs, activation functions are applied on the edges between nodes.
elements.sent-2411-362: Regarding activation functions, the two architectures also use different approaches.
elements.sent-2411-363: MLPs commonly use standard functions such as ReLU, defined as $\text{ReLU (z) = \max(0, z)$.}
elements.sent-2411-364: KANs employ the SiLU (Sigmoid Linear Unit) activation in combination with B-splines.
elements.sent-2411-365: For a fully connected layer with $d_{in}$ input neurons and $d_{out}$ output neurons, the number of learnable parameters \cite{yu2024kan is: \begin{equation}Parameters = (d_{in} × d_{out})+ d_{out}.\end{equation}.}
elements.sent-2411-366: FLOPs for forward propagation are therefore calculated as: \begin{equation}N_{FP} = (M + 2 M T) + (K-1)(M + 2 M^2) + (M + 2 M C). \label{eq:mlp_flops}\end{equation}.

elements.sent-2411-465: To achieve that, we first investigated the transferability of reference Kolmogorov-Arnold Networks (KANs) from regression to classification tasks , running a large number of models across 117 diverse datasets from the UCR Benchmark.
elements.sent-2411-466: We conducted hyperparameter search on two KAN implementations in view of finding the best architecture for the task at hand and concluded with an interpretability analysis.
elements.sent-2411-467: Our findings show that the Efficient KAN implementation has a significant performance improvement over the original KAN and is also superior to MLPs by 6 percentage points in F1 score on the reference architectures.
elements.sent-2411-468: The hyperparameter analysis revealed that Efficient KAN consistently outperforms KAN across variations in grid size, depth, and layer size, showcasing its robustness and adaptability to diverse datasets.
